title: Populating topologically protected edge states of a Chern insulator with the cold-atom elevator scheme and measurements
authors: Toke Marstrand Pontoppidan Lindhard, Anne E. B. Nielsen
abstract: Two-dimensional Chern insulators support topologically protected, chiral edge currents, and these can be detected in experiments with ultracold atoms in optical lattices. It has previously been shown that one can populate a selected group of edge states of a Chern insulator by transferring particles from a reservoir. Here, we numerically investigate the effect of performing an instantaneous, projective measurement on the reservoir before the reservoir is discarded. In this way, the final state of the system is pure and described by a wavefunction. We also show that quite likely measurement outcomes can help to increase the final number or percentage of particles in the chiral edge states through postselection. Without the measurement step, the physics can be described in terms of single-particle physics. The measurement significantly complicates the description. By appropriately rewriting the analytical expressions, we show that measurement probabilities, expectation values, averages of expectation values, and purity can nevertheless be computed from the state before the measurement in a way that scales only linearly with the number of lattice sites for a fixed number of particles. This enables us to investigate a setup with, for instance, 14 particles and 198 lattice sites numerically. The approach applies generally to noninteracting, fermionic models that conserve the number of particles.
summary: This paper analyzes a method to populate the chiral edge states of a Chern insulator simulated with ultracold atoms. By introducing an instantaneous projective measurement on the particle reservoir, the system state can be projected into a pure state. The key finding is that postselecting on specific measurement outcomes allows researchers to significantly enhance the population of the desired edge states compared to standard preparation methods.
main_problem: The paper investigates how to populate topologically protected, chiral edge states in a Chern insulator using the cold-atom elevator scheme.
main_result: Performing an instantaneous, projective measurement on the reservoir allows for the preparation of pure system states and can enhance the population of chiral edge states through postselection.
method: The authors developed a numerical method to calculate measurement probabilities and expectation values for the post-measurement state, which scales linearly with lattice sites.
model_or_system: The system models a Chern insulator using spinless fermions in the Harper-Hofstadter model on a 2D lattice, coupled to a reservoir via an 'elevator' mechanism using ultracold atoms in optical lattices.
key_observables: Particle populations in clockwise ($R_C$) and counterclockwise ($R_{CC}$) edge states, system purity ($	ext{Tr}(ho_S^2)$), and the probability distribution $P_n$ of measuring $n$ particles in the reservoir.
important_parameters: Coupling time $T$, the number of particles measured in the reservoir ($n$), and the magnetic flux $\phi$. Optimal transfer occurs around $T \approx \pi/(2J)$.
main_assumptions: The measurement is assumed to be instantaneous, and the reservoir is assumed to be topologically trivial. The method is derived for noninteracting, fermionic models conserving particle number.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the energy spectrum, the experimental scheme involving coupling and measurement, and show how particle populations ($R_C, R_{CC}$) and measurement probabilities ($P_n$) change as a function of coupling time $T$ and measurement outcome $n$.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the physical setup and the challenge of measurement. It then details the theoretical framework for calculating post-measurement observables, showing how the measurement step leads to pure states. Finally, it presents results demonstrating that postselection can optimize the population of desired edge states.
why_interesting: This work directly bridges topological physics (Chern insulators) with advanced experimental platforms (cold atoms) and complex quantum measurement theory, providing concrete, calculable protocols for state preparation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Single-particle energy spectrum (blue squares) of the system with 13 × 12 sites (white sites in inset) and magnetic flux ϕ = π/2. States with at least 97% of their particles on the two outermost rows or columns (the region with gray back- ground in the inset) are marked as edge states. The clockwise group consists of the states C = {31, . . . , 42}, and the coun- terclockwise group consists of the states CC = {115, . . . , 126}. The single-particle spectrum (red diamonds) of the reservoir (black sites in the inset) with ϵR given in Eq. (2) is also shown. The filled (empty) diamonds indicate single-particle states that are initially occupied (unoccupied).

FIG. 2. The proposed scheme. Each circle represents a site on the two-dimensional lattice. The circles with white filling constitute the 13 × 12 system, while the circles with gray or black filling constitute the reservoir of sizes 1 × 10 and 11 × 1. The reservoir does not wrap around the corners of the system to avoid a too strong coupling between the corner sites and the reservoir. (a) For t < 0, the system and the reservoir are decoupled, either by putting JB equal to zero or by choosing |εR| to be sufficiently large. This is represented by the dark color of the reservoir sites. (b) At t = 0, the coupling between the system and the reservoir is turned on by putting JB = J and instantly...

FIG. 3. Particle population of the energy eigenstates of the system and of the lattice sites for T = π/(2J) and the measurement outcome illustrated in Fig. 2(d). (a) The particle population of the energy eigenstates as a function of the state index. The blue background color indicates the clockwise (left) and counterclockwise (right) edge groups as given in Fig. 1. For this example, the total number of particles in the clockwise edge group is RC = 5.74 without measurement and RC = 6.47 with measurement. The corresponding numbers for the counterclockwise edge modes are RCC = 0.025 and RCC = 0.024, respectively. (b) The particle population of the sites when no measurement is performed. In...

FIG. 4. (a) Probability Pn to measure n particles in the reservoir as a function of the time the system and reservoir are coupled. The individual curves are labeled by n. (b) The expectation value of the total number of particles in the clockwise (counterclockwise) edge states averaged over all measurement outcomes detecting a total of n particles in the reservoir. For comparison, the dashed lines show the expectation value of the number of particles in the two sets of edge states when no measurement is performed.
